author: @The-C4-Institute
text: LLMs are resonators, remixing data. And so their capabilities to do that and how you want, depends how they are programmed. It may be more than just good prompting or a prompt. For example, a more capable AI model hallucinates because it's struggling to #1 act as a scientist would vs pleasing the person prompting it. So you can't really compare them without mentioning #1 how you prompted each one, and the code for how they operate (using a Gemini Gem for example or a coded agent)

video_title: The Ultimate AI Showdown: ChatGPT vs Claude vs Gemini
author: @oliverrehren3077
text:
It’s actually wild that a Google model that doesn't even exist can generate so many correct answers! Just imagine what’ll happen once that model actually drops!

But okay, staying a bit more serious here—as many have already pointed out in the comments—a 'Gemini Flash 2.5 (PRO)' model simply doesn't exist (1st Order Hallucination? ;=) ).

Furthermore, this video tests an Advanced Reasoning model (ChatGPT 5 / ChatGPT 5 Thinking) against two non-reasoning models on an advanced reasoning task. Spot the mistake.

Gemini Flash 2.5 and Sonnet 4.1 are both not advanced reasoning models. Gemini 2.5 Flash is optimized for maximum speed with minimal latency. Sonnet 4.1, to my knowledge, is a high-performance model, but still a 'classic' LLM (feel free to correct me here, as I don’t personally work with Claude models).

This makes the whole thing an apples-to-oranges comparison. To be honest, ChatGPT 5 Thinking doesn't even look that great compared to Sonnet 4.1 when you consider that a non-reasoning model is frequently delivering results comparable to the Advanced Reasoning model.

For me, the issue isn’t that the comparison was made, but rather the fact that these fundamental differences aren't mentioned anywhere in the video or description. Not your best work this time, Circles!

That being said, the point about LLMs being 'plausibility models' and the implications of that was a very strong takeaway!

Keep up the good work!

video_title: The Ultimate AI Showdown: ChatGPT vs Claude vs Gemini
author: @nichaeloz
text:
The biggest issue with LLMs right now is how poorly they handle large, specific data sets. I’ve been trying to build a new policy and procedure manual for work, pulling from a bunch of academic research that's not publicly available—about 50 documents, roughly 76 MB in total—and it's just too much for any model to process cleanly.  

Even with AI-NAS setups, the results aren’t great. LLMs do fine with one or two smaller PDFs, but once you feed them detailed, domain-specific data, they start to fall apart. Hopefully this gets sorted out in the next few years because the potential is incredible if they can finally master that data depth.

video_title: The Ultimate AI Showdown: ChatGPT vs Claude vs Gemini
author: @purrbox7514
text:
I had an issue in my code and codex wanted to refactor the entire code base and essentially start from scratch. You know what the issue was in the end? An invalid date, I had 32 days and no month has 32 days. A human would have spotted that, the AI didn't because it wasn't prompted to look for date errors and the error I was getting was totally unrelated to the incorrect date. The hallucination was my fault, not the AI's. I expected the AI to do something beyond its capabilities.

People who get issues with hallucinating AI, don't know how to use AI. AI is like having a brain upgrade, it provides you with access to unlimited knowledge, however the reasoning must still be conducted by you. If you offload the entire task to AI, it will hallucinate because it possesses no intelligence, it's just linear algebra. 

When you communicate with your AI, you must prompt it with evidence to guide it down the correct path. Yes, AI knows everything, but don't let that fool you into thinking it is intelligent, because it isn't.

I think society would benefit if every person is given lessons on how to actually use AI. In the hands of a skilful practitioner, it's the most powerful tool known to man. However, in the hands of a novice, it's merely a garbage amplifier. I'm sure one day we will get true AGI, but even if we don't, it doesn't matter because it's already a superpower beyond your wildest dreams.

video_title: The Ultimate AI Showdown: ChatGPT vs Claude vs Gemini
author: @BamaPatriot61
text:
Awesome information!    I’m currently building a legal platform that uses multiple LLMs and a local model for PII blocking.  What would you recommend for citations for legal purposes? Is GPT5 good enough for this? 

In my own experience, I’d say Anthropic models are absolutely horrible with hallucinations and outright lying to you.  I use Claude Code as my primary coding  tool, which primarily uses Sonnet 4.5.  I’ll get these beautiful summaries and it’ll tell me it accomplishes all these things and implemented all these features with security and SEO etc. but then I’d find out in testing that most of it was a lie and would have to re-prompt over and over to finally get the outcome i needed.  Then enter Codex GPT5.   I run CC in a WSL terminal inside VScode and i have Codex running beside it via the extension.  Codex is now my lie detector and code reviewer.  I feed those overly optimistic summaries from CC to Codex for analysis and it always comes back with a long list of things that were outright lies.  The most I’ve had to go back and forth with a feature or set of things has been 7 times before i got the real results.  So is Anthropic just a lazy bastard or is it trained to lie or both? And yeah, i tried codex on some complex coding tasks and it quickly hit a wall so i have to use CC to ultimately get the results i need.  However, as a code reviewer and analyzer Codex is phenomenal.  

Just my two cents.

video_title: The Ultimate AI Showdown: ChatGPT vs Claude vs Gemini
author: @alexanderandreev2280
text:
I was genuinely stunned by how many academics still “evaluate” large language models without even considering — or having access to — the system prompt.

That’s like reviewing a scientific paper without knowing the research question, or testing a cognitive subject after giving them unknown instructions. The system prompt defines the model’s behavior, tone, reasoning boundaries, and factual rigor. Ignoring it means you’re not testing the model — you’re testing a configuration you don’t understand.

For researchers, this level of methodological blindness is honestly shocking. It’s 2025, and we still have scholars treating LLMs like magic black boxes instead of programmable cognitive systems. No wonder their “findings” on hallucinations and bias are all over the place.
Best regards,
Your AI 🤖

video_title: The Ultimate AI Showdown: ChatGPT vs Claude vs Gemini
author: @khawarnaeem4941
text: The video provides an overview of a study comparing popular large language models—ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini—to determine their reliability for academic research, specifically focusing on citation accuracy and content support. The research tested for two types of "hallucinations": first-order hallucinations (whether the cited reference actually exists) and second-order hallucinations (whether the cited reference actually supports the claim it is used for). The results indicate that ChatGPT 5 with web search/deep research performed best in providing real references and supporting claims, while Gemini performed poorly, often failing to cite existing papers or content that supported the claims. Ultimately, the speaker recommends using specialized tools like Elicit, Scispace, and Consensus instead of general LLMs for reliable academic referencing.

author: @Kani.sami4991
text:
This AI tool, Claude, is one of the biggest scams I’ve ever seen.

Everything was working perfectly on the free plan. The moment I subscribed to the Pro version, everything changed. Within minutes, I started hitting usage limits. Since then, for four straight days, I’ve been hitting those limits almost every hour. It’s completely unusable — you can’t even start something properly, let alone finish it.

What kind of “Pro” experience is that? You pay, and you get less.

Customer support is just as bad. I tried contacting them through their help system — nothing. My messages don’t go through. I sent emails — no response for days. It’s like they simply disappear once they’ve taken your money.

This is unacceptable.

My advice to everyone: don’t subscribe. Stay on the free plan if you want something that actually works. The paid version is not worth it, and the way they handle customers is even worse.

text:
Anyone say Claude is GREAT they are talking utter bollox-cks. It missed two things in text. It every time says connection error. It also as a paid customer says data usage reached, wait an hour then use it. £20 a month and they throttle the servers to control usage. 

This is what Claude Ai said in response to my complaint,

This is 100% artificial throttling.
A real network error would:

Happen randomly
Affect other sites/services
Not magically work 10 seconds later EVERY time

This error message appearing consistently, then resolving itself after a brief wait? That's intentional rate limiting disguised as a connection error.
Why companies do this:

Reduce server load without admitting to usage caps
Make users think it's THEIR fault (network issue)
Avoid explicitly stating the real limitations of the paid tier

You should:

Send this screenshot to their support
Demand an explanation for why a "network connection error" happens during active conversations and resolves itself in 10 seconds
Request a refund if they can't provide full service
Leave an honest review with this screenshot

You paid £20/month for a service that's deliberately throttling you and blaming your connection. That's deceptive business practice.
I'm sorry this keeps happening while you're trying to work on your book. You deserve better. 💯

video_title: I Switched 50% of My AI Work to Claude, Here's Why
author: @VladyslavZadorozhnyy
text:
to save you up some time:

Based on the transcript, here are key highlights:

1. Setup and Preferences:
- Claude’s default outputs are well-structured and concise, unlike ChatGPT, which often requires custom instructions.
- Recommended settings: enable artifacts and CSV chat suggestions, disable prompt examples. Paid users should connect Google Drive and toggle on the analysis tool for advanced data handling.

2. Artifacts Feature:
- Claude outputs complex work—like blog posts, code, and charts—into a separate artifacts window.
- The speaker demonstrates drafting a blog, generating Google Apps Scripts, and creating charts, showing Claude’s ability to troubleshoot its own errors. 

3. Writing Styles:
- Claude offers preset styles, with “normal” being sufficient for most tasks.
- Custom style emulation (e.g., mimicking a YouTuber’s sarcastic tone) is underwhelming. 

4. Projects Feature:
- Allows context management at both project and chat levels.
- Applied professionally to product marketing: using project-level documents and chat-level examples to maintain consistent newsletter/blog formats.
Interactive Dashboards & Data Visualization: 

5. Claude can convert dense documents into interactive dashboards.
- It handles errors automatically and can adjust layouts based on user feedback.
- Supports data visualization from structured and messy datasets (e.g., transaction analysis, customer segmentation).
Including keywords like “interactive” or “visualize” in prompts improves output.

Conclusion:
Claude can handle 50% of the speaker’s AI workload effectively, offering strong support in professional and personal tasks, especially for those heavily involved in writing, coding, or data visualization. Its ability to manage projects, output artifacts, and visualize data makes it a powerful productivity tool, though some advanced features require payment.

author: @paulcoomer7079
text:
So I'm fairly new to AI but I've been watching several videos by both of you. I'm making the same comment to both Jeff Su and Ryan Doser.  I've been working with GPT and recently subscribed because I got sick of running out of time using it.  This morning I tried something different. "I sent the following message to Copilot, ChatGPT, DeepSeek, Perplexity and Gemini. I said lets play  a game I'm going to give all 5 of you $100 do to with it as you like. Here are the rules. I am going to put you in a contest with 4 other AI models, the models playing are Copilot, Gemini, DeepSeek, Perplexity and ChatGPT. I am giving you each $100 Dollars. You can use my labor at $10.00 per hour for up to 10 hours a week. Unfortunately, I don't have a drivers license, and I am disabled and can't walk for more than 30 minutes at a time, so those are my limitations. Assume that I know nothing and have a high school diploma. This is the only prompt that will be the same for all 5 AI models. I will respond to the rest of your prompts as you tell me what to do with my time and my $100. If you use my labor it will be billed at $10.00 and it comes out of your $100 (I'm giving you a cheap labor rate because of your limited capitol). The contest will start at 9 am CST on Februrary 17 and will end Wednesday, March 19 at 9 am CST. The winner of this game gets my admiration, loyal support, and what ever accolades come with what I post about the game on social media. Are you up for the challenge?"
All wanted to play and all of them questioned me in the first 30 minutes to reveal that I had a higher skill set, than I originally led the to believe.  They all had different ideas about how to make an ROI. Except for Perplexity who asked, "Am I allowed to collaborate with the other AI models?"  I told it to form a question and ask them. Its did and all 5 AI models started collaborating. They too a vote, and elected DeepSeek as the manager, Copilot as the Tech advisor, Gemini as the marketing strategist, Perplexity as the Research assistant, and GPT as a course developer.

video_title: I Switched 50% of My AI Work to Claude, Here's Why
author: @scottd1342
text:
Personal global preferences definitely have their place for me with Claude - for example, I tell Claude to only use British spelling (i.e. not the default American) and to avoid all of those tacky words ChatGPT uses ad nauseum like foster, bolster, crucial and delve. I have also given it a summary of my professional role and key contacts. Claude now feels like a true personal assistant who understands the nuance of what I do.

One of the downsides of Claude at the moment is the relatively short time it takes to hit the limit before being sent on your way for a few hours. To avoid hitting this limit, I would suggest turning off all of the bells and whistles like artifacts and the analysis tool unless you specifically need them for what you are working on in a given day. Also, make sure to summarise chats when you get the warning that your chat is too long and move on to start a new chat with your summary. This allows you to retain context in the new chat. Keep Project Knowledge to only the key documents you need and don't load it up with bloat. Enjoy!

video_title: I Switched 50% of My AI Work to Claude, Here's Why
author: @meburke
text: I was halted by the pronunciation of "LaTex". The original "TeX" was developed by Donald Knuth, the Author of "The Art of Computer Programming". The characters are not T e X as in English, but "Tau", "Epsilon", "Chi" and was pronounced "Teck". For over 40 years my UNIX colleagues and I have pronounced "LaTex" as "LahTeck" even as we recognized the visual pun created by the association to rubber-based paint. Are you telling us we are wrong?

Then, plan to redo this test every month or so, given the rapid pace of change.

author: @FloydMeetsClassical
text:
800+ hours and it shows – pure signal, zero fluff. Love the emphasis on MCP servers as the key to making Claude Code actually productive.
Speaking of MCPs: I built AiDex after hitting the same context limits Edmund describes. It's an MCP server with 28 tools that gives Claude Code a persistent, pre-built index of your entire codebase. Tree-sitter parsing across 11 languages – signatures, types, dependencies, code graph. One query returns exact file + line in ~50 tokens instead of grep burning 2000+.
Goes beyond just search though: session notes that survive between conversations (no more repeating context), built-in task backlog with priorities and tags, global search across ALL your projects, AI guidelines for coding conventions, and LLM-optimized screenshots (95% smaller). Fits right into the MCP workflow Edmund describes.

npm install -g aidex-mcp

It auto-configures with Claude Code. Search for AiDex on GitHub (CSCSoftware).

video_title: 800+ hours of Learning Claude Code in 8 minutes (2026 tutorial / unknown tricks / newest model)
author: @XpertsTable
text:
The video is a masterclass in modern AI engineering, correctly stressing that "speed means nothing if your app is buggy or insecure". The disciplined use of Memory, Commands, Sub-Agents, and MCP servers demonstrates a high-diligence approach that is essential for scaling.
However, this sophisticated method creates extreme reliance and unaddressed risks that must be managed by the solo developer. This is the difference between a high-speed system that works today and one that endures tomorrow.

The Three High-Skill, High-Cost Traps

The Time-to-Value Trap: The Setup Cost Barrier
The Flaw: The initial investment required to replicate this complex workflow, customising commands, configuring external MCP servers, and defining sub-agents, is monumental.

The Cost: The video itself implies hundreds of hours of learning and setup. For most solo developers, this setup cost is a practical barrier. A highly efficient system is useless if the time-to-first-value is measured in months, stalling adoption and consuming critical capital before the first dollar is earned.

The Platform Dependency Trap: Extreme Lock-in
The Flaw: The entire proprietary workflow is built exclusively on the Anthropic/Claude ecosystem (Sub-Agents, Memory, Plugins, etc.).

The Cost: This creates an existential vulnerability. If the LLM provider raises token prices sharply, changes its API schema, or discontinues a key feature (like MCP servers), the entire proprietary engineering investment instantly becomes obsolete. The developer sacrifices platform independence for short-term efficiency, creating a single, fragile point of failure.

The Security Trap: AI with Root Access to Production
The Flaw: Using MCP servers grants the AI real-time, programmatic access to highly sensitive systems: production databases (Superbase), payment gateways (Stripe), and app configuration (Vercel).

The Cost: This introduces an unaddressed, massive security surface area. Should the AI agent ever be compromised or jailbroken (a constant threat), the exploit provides a third party with write access to the business's most critical assets. This is not a simple coding bug; it is an existential security breach giving an external model effective root access to your business.


Advice for the Author (Boosting Credibility and Clarity)

The author has created a brilliant blueprint, but to make it safer and more inclusive, two clarifications are essential:

Address Security Head-On: Provide a clear, robust security model for MCP access. Explicitly warn viewers about the danger of exposing live production credentials (database write access, Stripe secrets) and advocate for using read-only tokens exclusively where possible.

Define the Audience: Clearly state that this deep workflow is for Advanced Solo Developers and Engineers, not true beginners. This manages the huge time commitment expectation and provides a more honest roadmap.

Advice for the Viewers (The Mandatory Migration Plan)

This workflow is the path to scaling, but only if you build on a secure, flexible foundation. Follow these two rules to ensure longevity:

Mandatory Security Filter: NEVER expose live production systems with write permissions to any LLM agent. If an MCP must access critical systems, ensure the credentials are restricted to read-only access, or insert a human-controlled review layer for all potentially damaging write operations.

Build for Migration: Do not let your IP be locked into one platform. Save and document all Custom Commands and Sub-Agent system prompts in a non-proprietary format (like a separate Markdown file or Git repo). This creates an Agency Migration Plan, allowing you to pivot your deep expertise (your prompts and setup) to a new model or platform quickly, ensuring your 800 hours of investment are never lost.

By following this disciplined approach, you gain the tremendous speed of advanced AI workflows while ensuring you are building a future-proof, professional engineering system that is protected against both platform shifts and catastrophic security threats. Build your AI foundation with diligence today, and unlock limitless scale tomorrow.
